speaker: interviewer
text: Three The date is May 7th, 1996. The survivor is Boris Chartan. The interview is taking place in Plainview, New York, United States of America. I'm interviewing him. My name is Anne Bernard. And the interview is taking place in English. Let's go back to what you were describing when you met your father in Sasow, the labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I met my father and Sasow in the labor camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two of my cousins were there too, young boys. And we all slept just on bunkers, like type spread out close to one another. I was able to stay near my dad, which made me feel warm, I mean, to be near him. And he asked me how mom was doing. He hasn't seen her. I told him what had happened. And we just went to work every day. We were taken out in the morning, marched about three kilometers, about close to 700 Jews, again mining the mountains around that area for the roads. And all we were given there is some watery soup for lunch. And then towards the evening, marched back, and constantly under the police of Ukrainians and German. The camp was Heinz Montek, in Sasow, that I recall his name, which was the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any personal dealings with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never had any personal dealings with him. The only one that had personal dealings with him later on was my dad. My dad used to-- he lived off the camp. And the duty, later on, my dad was assigned was to go to clean his house every day. So when everybody went to work to the mountains, my dad would be sent up there, and spend the whole day around the house cleaning, and working, and doing that sort of stuff for him. And I guess he must have say hello to him. And that's about all. And he's never hit my dad or anything. And this is one of the reasons why my dad is alive, because, as this Polish man was bringing food every once in a while, Mr. Marczeniek, my dad had kept some merchandise that he had from the store, and trusted it with Marczeniek. And he had asked the SS, Mr. Montek, whether he would like to get some-- to make himself some nice boots, some material or everything. And he said yes. So the next package that this Polish man brought there was leather for the Mr. Montek. And by befriending him like this, my dad's story was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one day when he arrived at the house to clean, he said to him that on the next day, when you ascend here, you can go free, if you want. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My dad came back to camp. And he quietly told some of the people, not that he's running away. But that he has a feeling that the camp will be liquidated at a near future, just giving them the inkling. That next morning when my dad came to the house, he-- this SS Montek said to him, you're free to go now, if you choose. And the words were to him, he says, [GERMAN]. I hope that you will be able, in essence, survive the war. My father chose to-- at that point, not to go back to camp from the house, which was probably maybe half a mile. And he just went into the woods, and started walking back towards this Polish village, Palikrowy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in the year of 19-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] early '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back a little bit before you're-- when you first got to the camp, and you were with your father and cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe, in somewhat more detail, the sleeping arrangements? Did you have blankets and pillows?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no blankets and pillows. There was just the wooden platform, some straw on a platform. You slept almost literally in your clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the winter, how did you keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just staying close to one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did more than one person share a bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, there wasn't a separate bunk. There was just straight platform. And everybody just laid on it. Upper and lower.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever get sick in these bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that winter, I wasn't-- I was [INAUDIBLE] already having left. A lot of the people started to get typhus from the uncleanliness, running a very high temperature. And the-- those that were very weak were taken out and shot, killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know this?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my dad. I've witnessed some of them. I've seen it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in camp, Sasow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this happened before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I left, yes. People were getting sick daily there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel during this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was frightening. It was very frightening, because typhus is also contagious. And you were just frightened that, who's next? And how weak are you going to be?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel weak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I didn't get sick. I did not have typhus in Sasow. I did have typhus after-- right after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left to go to Lackie, was that in the winter or the summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the winter, in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far away was it from Sasow?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 kilometers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was just another labor camp that they had set up there. And there were people-- in Sasow, you had people from Podkamien, from [POLISH]. Sasow-- people-- there was a [? town ?] Sasow. And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] mostly from all the villages in the surrounding area. When you came to Zlotki, already you had people from-- as far back as Lwow and [POLISH], different people from different towns that were brought in there to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people would you estimate were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sasow?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lackie.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lackie, there was probably about 1,100 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work on the road, mainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fixing the roads.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By hand, with shovels. Most of the roads were dirt roads we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did they need fixing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- because of the severe winters that you have there, and all the heavy equipment that started coming through from the German army. That's what really kept-- they had to keep up. Otherwise, they know that-- they were afraid they're not going to be able to move the artillery and machinery through these roads. So they kept us working to keep up with the roads.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe actually what you did to fix these roads?

speaker: interviewee
text: You'd have what you call a barrel, push it with dirt, with stones, some gravel, and just keep patching potholes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you smooth the potholes?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- we did not have a roller like you see here. We'd have something that you just stand and bang it in by hand, all handmade stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of numbers of people were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe hundreds, just every-- every 30, 40 feet, there'd be another group working, just along the whole road. And the police would just stand over you, watch you, so you don't run away or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women doing this too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Those labor-- the two labor camps, there was very little women. The women that were doing-- they were doing work in the camp itself, maybe in the kitchen, that sort of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get word about your mother, who was back in Podkamien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from the Polish man that used to come up. He didn't come to Lackie to me. So I didn't get any word.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Lackie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lackie, I was exactly six to seven weeks. And I made up my mind to run away. And When they were in marching us down back from work one evening, and it was just about sunset. And they used to stop us after they marched us, let's say, two miles. They'd say, OK, stop, everybody sit down in the fields. And next to it, there was a forest. And I just-- I don't know what got into my mind or what. And I just took off, and run right in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started running, and walking from village to village, working my way back to [NON-ENGLISH]. And it took me probably four to five days, maybe even more. I don't actually recollect. I'd stop someplace, and go into a farm, and beg for some food. And I'd get it, luckily. Sometimes, you got nothing. And just try to stay out of the main roads, just because there was a lot of forest there that would interconnect between fields and forest. And I had a pretty good idea where it-- you'd ask once in a while, how to get to the next village, or the next village, till I worked my way back. When I came back, I came back to Podkamien. And my mom and dad were there, hidden already, they couldn't walk around freely, because at that point already it was almost Judenfrei at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through this Marczeniek. I know enough to contact this Polish man. There was still some Jews hidden in the town, because there was a group that kept working it with [INAUDIBLE]. It was not a labor camp, but just a group of people that they kept them working. And the Ukrainian police was watching over them. They'd have to go every day to work, and do this type of work, whether it was cleaning the police stations, or doing road work, or whatever. There wasn't too much-- it was a small group that was kept back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found your parents on this farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And at that point, we did not really make up-- or put it better, we were not offered by Mr. Marczeniek to come and stay there. It was frightening, I guess, because they would have killed him if they knew he was harboring Jews, hiding Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the year of-- that was in '43, just before the high holidays. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We all embarked into a forest, a group of Jews. And we figured that we can hide out in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got there, some of my cousins were there-- somebody of the Olaska family, and the Stein family, and the Horowitz family, and my father's two brothers with their family. It was in the forest towards a Polish village which was a little more friendlier than the Ukrainian type of villages. So we were able to go in the evening and get some food from them, whether you bought it or they gave it to you. And we'd cook in the forest, make a little fire, try to keep the fire very low, because of the Germans cannot see the smoke coming out, because otherwise you'd give away where you are. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what I recall is the high holidays spending there, and all of us standing wrapped up and praying, saying [NON-ENGLISH] in the darkness of the night. And the Rabbi was there. He was alive. His name was Yitzak Homilen. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And we went through [NON-ENGLISH]. After that, my father says that there's no way we're going to be able to survive here. At night, a few times we were assailed by Ukrainians. They didn't kill us then. They just robbed us. Whatever little possessions you still had, you held on to. And they ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at one point on a Friday night, my dad says, we better get out of here, and go back to see if we can talk to Mr. Marczeniek that he would take us in permanently. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We started walking. In order to go to-- and this was-- the village name was [POLISH]. We had to go to [POLISH]. We had to cross a road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was coming out of this forest. There was a little field, a road, and then back into a forest. And it was Friday night. It must have been 8:00, 9:00 o'clock at night, best I collect. And as soon as we stepped out on the main road, I recall we heard voices of the Ukrainian police. And I only could see through the side of my eyes when he pulled a shotgun. But he wasn't too close to us. We all spread. We ran back into the forest. I did not see my mother, and my father. And it was just frightening. And I was running. And being at that time of the year, the forest was very dry. So you could hear when you run, you keep breaking the branches. And as I was running, just running, when I heard these cracks. And it's pitch dark. All I yelled is, mama, mama, papa. My father answered. It was him running too. Somehow or other, we ran into one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sat down. And we could not find my mother until daybreak, Saturday daybreak. We thought already that we lost her.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started walking and walking. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we met another Jewish family that was hiding in the forest. And they told us where Mama is. And we went over and we hooked up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this time when we hooked up, we stayed there over Saturday, and Sunday. And Monday, we went to Palikrowy, to that village, to Mr. Marczeniek. And he took us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad spoke to him. And he made arrangements that we're going to stay there. He built a bunker to hide us in there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was under a Haystack that we dug, and dug. And it had to be built.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of you went into the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we went in just the three of us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we learned that my grandfather Hirsch Milgrom was still alive. We had sent-- Mr. Marczeniek went and got him, and brought him to stay with us. So there was four of us in that bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you stand in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you could not stay, just about on your knees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to stay there all day. We'd get out at night, just to stretch out and do a little walking around. It was a farm. He was a very rich farmer. He had a beautiful farm. Him and his wife, they had no children. And he was a very, very dear friend to my dad. They used to try to bring us into the house at night, late at night. And she was a very devout Catholic. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a priest used to come there from time to time. And the priest knew that he harbored us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's the only one that knew, nobody else. And we stayed there through 19-- late '43 into '44. I'd say, we must have stayed there, [INAUDIBLE], that would be say September, October, November, December, January, February, up to March, just about seven months. They fed us, they kept us. They were wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you breathe in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the open-- the open hole where you came through. There was no other way of getting air. There's only once that the Ukrainians came there to look for Jews. We didn't know whether it was somebody squealed or what, that somebody got wind of it. But thank God, they was-- they didn't find us. They just weren't smart enough, because he had stacked it up. The harvest is in the summer. He had stacked it up so much with hay, that no matter how much jumping they were going to-- there would be no inkling that it was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never left there. We were-- the only time-- but we left his place already was when the Russians came back when we were freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liberated from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way were you liberated? How did that come to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a twofold thing. But in between, a cousin of mine Junior Olaska, which also survived, she's in Israel, lost-- all these relatives of mine were killed in February, the rest of them. Whoever was around the forest were all wiped out, and a village by the name of [POLISH], almost that village where my grandfather lived in. She ran. She was lucky. She ran away. And she ran in to a Polish house. And the woman took her in. The woman's name is Mrs. Madinska. She was given the righteous award from [INAUDIBLE]. She lives in Connecticut. We got a message to us that they wiped everybody you. The only one that is left is her. Two of my father's brothers were still alive, Pinchas Chartan and Shmuel Chartan. But we didn't know where they both are. What my father did is for Mr. Marczeniek, we had sent-- we entrusted another Polish young man. He must have been in his twenties. And he swore to secrecy that he was not going to do any harm or tell anybody. He went and got her, and brought her to us. That was in either late January or early February, to our bunker. We took her in. And that's the way she survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In March, what had happened is on a Sunday, you could hear that the front were coming closer, the shooting that went on between the Germans and the Russians. The Russians were advancing. Of course, we were-- Mr. Marczeniek fed us all this, because we couldn't see it or hear it. We were not communicating with anything. And he says, he thinks that it's almost the end is coming, that we will be freed shortly. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We were afraid. He was afraid that they were going to burn the village, because he heard what they were doing in other villages, the Germans. He was afraid that we might get burned in that thing, because he was a rich Polish farmer. And they may take it out on him at the end. We went from his farmhouse into the forest, which was maybe 1,000 feet away, Sunday morning. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some Germans cavalry spotted us. And they started chasing after us. But luckily, there was a ravine. And it was in March. It was very muddy and everything. Their horses got into mud. And they couldn't catch us. On foot, we did better. And we, again, ran into the forest. And we survived. And it was that-- that was the Sunday before. So, finally, nothing happened to the village. We came back Sunday night. We returned to the farm. And we had dinner. And, again, he took us in there, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] talked to us about it, what had happened. And he says, it's imminent that it's-- they could see by the movement and everything. The following Sunday, the Ukrainians assembled all Pollocks into that ravine. And one of them was his wife. And she was killed that Sunday afternoon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandfather almost went to that ravine, but some Pollock said to him, they don't want Jews. They recognized him because he had a beard and this. He immediately ran into a stable and hid himself. It was a miracle. It was a miracle of God. And Sunday night, the-- Mr. Marczeniek came and told us what had happened to his wife, because we were very sad. I recall going with my dad, picking up her body out of the ravine. There was hundreds of Polish killed and shot dead. And we buried her on Monday ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that they knew that she was collaborating and protecting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it had nothing to do with it, no. That was not why she was-- they just-- the Ukrainians were always-- like the Bosnians, and the Muslims. And that's the reason they just picked to wipe out the Poles at the end. They figured this is their last hurrah that they could do. And by Thursday, we were-- we were liberated. We saw the Russian army come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sad, happy, and sad, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had an idea that there's nobody left. I mean, just a few of us, you could be happy for yourself. But still, it-- you lost [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] generations of Jews, family, friends.
